name: Full-Stack Web Development Bootcamp
blurb: A 12-week immersive bootcamp taking beginners from HTML to a deployed MERN-stack application.
text:
Full-Stack Web Development Bootcamp — 12-Week Immersive Program

An intensive, project-based program that takes students from first principles to a deployed full-stack application. Cohorts meet full-time; each week pairs lectures with hands-on labs and a graded build.

Week 1 — Web Foundations & HTML: How the web works (HTTP, DNS, the request/response cycle), semantic HTML5, forms, and accessibility basics. Tools: VS Code, Chrome DevTools. Project: static multi-page marketing site.

Week 2 — CSS & Responsive Design: The box model, Flexbox, CSS Grid, custom properties, media queries, and mobile-first layout. Tools: Sass, Google Fonts. Project: responsive landing page from a Figma mockup.

Week 3 — Version Control with Git: Git internals, branching, merging, resolving conflicts, pull requests, and collaborative workflow on GitHub. Project: team feature branch exercise.

Week 4 — JavaScript Fundamentals: Variables, data types, functions, arrays, objects, loops, and ES6+ syntax (arrow functions, destructuring, spread/rest, modules). Project: command-line-style logic challenges.

Week 5 — DOM & Async JavaScript: DOM selection and manipulation, events, the event loop, callbacks, Promises, async/await, and the Fetch API. Project: interactive weather dashboard consuming a public REST API.

Week 6 — React Fundamentals: Components, JSX, props, state, and the useState hook. Tools: Vite, React Developer Tools. Project: to-do application with component composition.

Week 7 — Advanced React: useEffect, the Context API, controlled forms, lifting state, and client-side routing with React Router. Project: multi-page movie browser with search and filtering.

Week 8 — Node.js & Express: The Node runtime, npm, building REST APIs, routing, middleware, error handling, and environment variables with dotenv. Project: RESTful notes API.

Week 9 — MongoDB & Mongoose: NoSQL data modeling, schemas, CRUD operations, relationships, and MongoDB Atlas. Tools: Compass, Postman. Project: persist the notes API to a database.

Week 10 — Authentication & Security: Password hashing with bcrypt, JSON Web Tokens (JWT), protected routes, authorization middleware, and CORS. Project: user signup/login with session-protected endpoints.

Week 11 — Full-Stack Integration & Deployment: Connecting a React front end to an Express/MongoDB back end (the MERN stack), managing state across the stack, and deploying to Render and Netlify with environment configuration. Project: deployed full-stack app.

Week 12 — Capstone: Students design, build, and present an original full-stack MERN application incorporating authentication, a database, and a deployed live URL. Emphasis on code review, documentation, and demo-day presentation to hiring partners.

Graduates leave with a deployed portfolio project, a version-controlled GitHub history, and job-search coaching.

name: Data Science & Machine Learning Bootcamp
blurb: A 16-week immersive covering Python, pandas/NumPy, SQL, statistics, scikit-learn, visualization, and intro deep learning with TensorFlow/Keras.
text:
Data Science & Machine Learning Bootcamp — 16-Week Syllabus

Format: Full-time, project-based. Tools installed via Anaconda; work in Jupyter Notebook, JupyterLab, VS Code, and Git/GitHub. Capstone presented in Week 16.

Weeks 1–2 — Python Foundations for Data
Core Python: data types, lists, dicts, comprehensions, functions, error handling, virtual environments (venv, conda). File I/O with CSV and JSON. Intro to NumPy: ndarray creation, vectorized operations, broadcasting, slicing, random sampling. Project: build a command-line data-cleaning utility.

Weeks 3–4 — Data Wrangling with pandas
Series and DataFrames, indexing (loc/iloc), groupby, merge/join, pivot tables, handling missing data, datetime parsing, method chaining. Reading from CSV, Excel, and APIs with requests. Project: clean and reshape a messy real-world sales dataset.

Week 5 — SQL for Data Science
Relational modeling, SELECT/WHERE/JOIN, GROUP BY, subqueries, window functions, CTEs. Hands-on with PostgreSQL and SQLite; querying from Python via SQLAlchemy and pandas.read_sql. Project: analytics queries against a normalized e-commerce schema.

Weeks 6–7 — Statistics & Probability
Descriptive statistics, distributions, central limit theorem, sampling, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing (t-tests, chi-square, ANOVA), correlation, p-values and pitfalls. A/B test design and analysis. Tools: NumPy, SciPy (scipy.stats), statsmodels. Project: run and interpret an A/B test.

Week 8 — Data Visualization
Matplotlib fundamentals, seaborn statistical plots, Plotly for interactivity. Principles of effective charts, EDA workflows. Project: exploratory data analysis notebook with a visual narrative.

Weeks 9–11 — Machine Learning with scikit-learn
Supervised learning: linear and logistic regression, k-NN, decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting (XGBoost). Preprocessing with Pipeline and ColumnTransformer, StandardScaler, one-hot encoding. Model selection: train/test split, cross-validation, GridSearchCV, evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision/recall, ROC-AUC, RMSE). Unsupervised learning: k-means, PCA. Project: end-to-end classification model with tuned hyperparameters.

Week 12 — Feature Engineering & Model Interpretation
Feature scaling, encoding strategies, handling imbalanced data (SMOTE), feature importance, SHAP values. Regularization (Lasso/Ridge). Project: improve a baseline model through feature work.

Weeks 13–14 — Introduction to Deep Learning
Neural network fundamentals, TensorFlow and Keras Sequential API, dense networks, activation functions, backpropagation, loss functions, optimizers (Adam), overfitting and dropout. Brief look at CNNs for image classification on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST. Project: train a Keras classifier and evaluate.

Week 15 — Time Series & Applied Analytics
Trend/seasonality decomposition, rolling windows, ARIMA with statsmodels, forecasting basics. Recommender concepts. Project: forecasting notebook.

Week 16 — Capstone
Individual end-to-end project: problem framing, data acquisition, cleaning, EDA, modeling, evaluation, and a presented findings deck. Portfolio published to GitHub.

Note: This program focuses on analysis and modeling fundamentals. It does not cover cloud platforms (AWS SageMaker), Spark or big-data processing, MLOps and model deployment, experiment tracking, or containerization with Docker.

name: University CS Core (Years 1-2)
blurb: Traditional two-year CS core: Java, C, DSA, discrete math, SQL, OS, and networking; light on web, cloud, and DevOps.
text:
YEAR 1 — SEMESTER 1

CS101 Programming Fundamentals (Java). Wk 1-4: JDK setup in Eclipse, variables, primitive types, control flow, methods, arrays. Wk 5-8: object-oriented basics — classes, objects, encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, interfaces. Wk 9-12: exception handling, file I/O, unit testing with JUnit, intro GUIs in JavaFX. Project: console inventory management system.

MA110 Discrete Mathematics. Propositional and predicate logic, proof techniques (induction, contradiction), set theory, relations, functions, combinatorics, graph theory, recurrence relations, Big-O notation. Proofs typeset in LaTeX.

YEAR 1 — SEMESTER 2

CS102 Systems Programming (C). Wk 1-4: C syntax, pointers, the memory model, arrays and strings, compilation with gcc and Make. Wk 5-8: dynamic memory (malloc/free), structs, linked lists; debugging with GDB and memory profiling with Valgrind. Wk 9-12: bit manipulation, the preprocessor, low-level file handling. Project: custom memory allocator plus a CLI text-processing utility.

YEAR 2 — SEMESTER 1

CS201 Data Structures & Algorithms (Java). Wk 1-3: asymptotic analysis, arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues. Wk 4-6: recursion, sorting (quicksort, mergesort, heapsort). Wk 7-9: binary search trees, AVL trees, heaps, hash tables. Wk 10-12: graph traversal (BFS/DFS), Dijkstra's shortest path, dynamic programming, greedy algorithms. Assignments graded on an automated online judge.

CS202 Object-Oriented Design. UML class and sequence diagrams, design patterns (Singleton, Observer, Factory, Strategy), SOLID principles, refactoring. Implemented in Java using IntelliJ IDEA.

YEAR 2 — SEMESTER 2

CS210 Database Systems. Relational model, ER modeling, normalization (1NF through BCNF), SQL DDL/DML, joins, subqueries, aggregation, indexing, transactions and ACID properties. Hands-on with PostgreSQL and MySQL; application access via JDBC. Project: normalized schema design with a query and reporting suite.

CS220 Operating Systems. Processes and threads, CPU scheduling, synchronization (mutexes, semaphores, deadlock avoidance), memory management, paging, virtual memory, file systems. Labs in C on Linux using POSIX threads (pthreads) and system calls. Project: a multithreaded command-line shell.

CS230 Computer Networks. OSI and TCP/IP layers, Ethernet, IP addressing and subnetting, routing, TCP vs UDP, DNS, HTTP fundamentals. Socket programming with the Berkeley sockets API in C; packet capture and analysis in Wireshark. Project: client-server chat application over TCP sockets.

CS240 Software Engineering. Software development life cycle, requirements engineering, Agile/Scrum, version control with Git, code review, testing strategies, UML modeling. Capstone: a team-built desktop application.

Note: advanced electives (web development, cloud computing, distributed systems) are offered in Years 3-4 and fall outside this core sequence.
